2603.21350.title: Beyond Memorization: Distinguishing between Reductive and Epistemic Reasoning in LLMs using Classic Logic Puzzles
2603.21350.paper_summary: Epistemic reasoning requires agents to infer the state of the world from partial observations and information about other agents' knowledge. Prior work evaluating LLMs on canonical epistemic puzzles interpreted their behavior through a dichotomy between epistemic reasoning and brittle memorization. We argue that this framing is incomplete: in recent models, memorization is better understood as a special case of reduction, where a new instance is mapped onto a known problem. Instead, we introduce a reduction ladder, a sequence of modifications that progressively move instances away from a canonical epistemic puzzle, making reduction increasingly difficult while preserving the underlying logic. We find that while some large models succeed via reduction, other models fail early, and all models struggle once epistemic reasoning is required.
2603.21350.paper_summary_zh: 認知推理要求代理人根據部分觀察和其他代理人知識的信息推斷世界的狀態。<lb/>先前的工作在評估大型語言模型（LLMs）對於經典認知難題的表現時，通過認知推理和脆弱記憶之間的二分法來解釋它們的行為。<lb/>我們認為這種框架是不完整的：在最近的模型中，記憶更好地被理解為一種特殊的簡化情況，其中一個新實例被映射到一個已知問題上。<lb/>相反，我們引入了一個簡化階梯，這是一系列逐步修改的過程，將實例逐漸遠離經典認知難題，使得簡化變得越來越困難，同時保持基本邏輯。<lb/>我們發現，雖然一些大型模型通過簡化取得成功，但其他模型則早期失敗，並且所有模型在需要認知推理時都會遇到困難。
2603.21350.author: Adi Gabay et.al.
2603.21350.authors: Adi Gabay, Gabriel Stanovsky, Liat Peterfreund
